turns.text: Building your 90-minute exam on score-based models, diffusion, and flow matching — here's a balanced mix of conceptual depth and calculation problems.
follow_ups: Make it harder with more derivations

suggestions.title: Generative Models Exam — Score-Based, Diffusion & Flow Matching

params.additional_instructions: Weight distribution: ~7 questions on score-based models & NCSN (forward process, true score, loss, ALD), ~7 on diffusion models (DDPM forward/reverse, noise schedules, posterior, sampling), ~6 on flow matching and score-based vs. diffusion trade-offs. Include 2–3 calculation problems requiring formula application (e.g., step size, loss computation) and 3–4 conceptual reasoning questions on when to use each approach.

artifact.title: AI623 — Deep Vision Language Models: Score-Based Models & Diffusion Exam

questions.text: Write down the formula for the forward diffusion process that produces a noise-perturbed data point x̃ from a clean data point x and a noise vector ε sampled from a standard normal distribution. Define each variable in your formula.
Explain what the noise level σ represents in the forward diffusion process, and describe how increasing σ changes the relationship between the original data point x and the corrupted data point x̃.
Given a clean image pixel x = 0.5, a noise level σ = 0.2, and a sampled noise vector ε = 0.3, calculate the resulting corrupted pixel value x̃ using the forward diffusion process formula.
In the context of the forward diffusion process, explain what is meant by the 'corrupted data distribution' and what role the noise standard deviation σ plays in defining this distribution.

You are designing a noise schedule for the forward diffusion process. If you want to produce a heavily corrupted version of an image with significant noise, should you use a small or large value of σ? Justify your answer with reference to the noise injection mechanics.

Write down the formula for the true score of the Gaussian distribution S_true(x̃ | x, σ), where x̃ is the noise-perturbed data point, x is the clean data, ϵ is the noise vector that was added, and σ² is the variance of the noise level.

Given a clean image x, noise vector ϵ ~ N(0, I) with standard deviation σ = 0.3, and perturbed image x̃ = x + 0.3ϵ, compute the true score S_true(x̃ | x, σ) in terms of ϵ and σ. Simplify your final expression.

A neural network predicts S_θ(x̃, σ) for a noise level σ. Write the formula for the unweighted NCSN loss (Denoising Score Matching loss) that measures the squared error between the network's prediction and the true score S_true(x̃ | x, σ).

Explain why the weighted NCSN loss multiplies the unweighted squared error term by λ(σ) = σ². In particular, discuss what would happen to training if all noise levels were weighted equally instead (i.e., no λ factor), and why noise-conditional score networks benefit from this balancing.

A score network outputs S_θ(x̃, σ) = [−0.2, 0.1] at noise level σ = 0.5 for a particular image patch. The true noise that was added was ϵ = [0.1, −0.05]. Compute the unweighted loss for this prediction. Show all working.

Explain the difference between the unweighted NCSN loss and the weighted NCSN loss. Why is the weighting factor λ(σ) = σ² necessary in the training objective, and what practical problem does it solve?

You are training a Noise-Conditional Score Network at noise level σ = 0.5. The network predicts S_θ(x̃, σ) = [0.2, -0.1, 0.3] and the true score is -(ϵ/σ²) = [0.1, -0.2, 0.4]. Calculate both the unweighted loss and the weighted loss with λ(σ) = σ². Show your work.

In the weighted NCSN loss formula L(θ) = λ(σ)σ||S_θ(x̃, σ) + ϵ||²₂ with λ(σ) = σ², analyze how the total weighting factor σ³ affects gradient flow during backpropagation at very small and very large noise levels. What imbalance would occur without this weighting, and how does it help?

Given a noise schedule with noise levels σ₁ = 0.1, σ₂ = 0.5, and σ₃ = 1.0, and squared prediction errors ||S_θ(x̃, σᵢ) - S_true||²₂ of 0.04, 0.04, and 0.04 respectively at each level, compute the weighted loss contribution from each noise level using λ(σ) = σ². Which noise level contributes most to the total gradient, and why?

Compare the training dynamics when using the unweighted NCSN loss versus the weighted loss. Under the unweighted formulation, would the network tend to learn faster at high or low noise levels? Explain how the σ² weighting factor corrects this bias and why balanced learning across all noise levels is important for score-based model quality.

A practitioner trains a score network using only the unweighted NCSN loss without noise-level balancing. During sampling with Annealed Langevin Dynamics, they observe that the model performs poorly at recovering clean images from highly corrupted states but performs well at low noise levels. Explain why this failure pattern occurs and how switching to the weighted loss with λ(σ) = σ² would address

The forward process in NCSN generates a noisy sample via x̃ = x + σϵ, where ϵ ~ N(0, I). Explain how the true score function S_θ^true(x̃|x) = -ϵ/σ² emerges as the natural training target when the network must reverse this corruption. In your answer, discuss how the relationship between the added noise ϵ, the noise level σ, and the score's dependency on variance σ² reflects the geometry of the cond

You are training an NCSN and must compute the loss for a single training step. Your network predicts S_θ(x̃, σ) for a corrupted sample x̃ at noise level σ. The true score is S_θ^true(x̃|x) = -ϵ/σ². If the weighting scheme λ(σ) = σ² is applied (as per the simplified weighted objective), derive the final weighted loss L(θ) and explain why multiplying by σ² is not merely a scaling convenience but ref

Consider the Annealed Langevin Dynamics (ALD) update rule: x̃^t = x̃^(t-1) + α_i S_θ(x̃^(t-1), σ_i) + √(2α_i) Z_t, where α_i = ϵ_step · σ_i² / σ_L² is the adaptive step size. Critically evaluate why the step size scales with σ_i² (the current noise variance) divided by σ_L² (the smallest noise variance). What would happen if the step size were held constant across all noise levels instead? Discuss

The DDPM-to-NCSN conversion formula states that S_θ(x_t, t) = -ϵ_θ(x_t, t) / √(1 - ᾱ_t), where ϵ_θ is a DDPM noise prediction and ᾱ_t is the cumulative variance schedule product. Analyze how this formula bridges the two model families: explain what the denominator √(1 - ᾱ_t) represents in terms of noise accumulation, and why dividing the predicted noise by this factor converts a noise-prediction o

answer_key.answer: x̃ = x + σε, where x is the clean data point, σ is the noise level (standard deviation), ε is a sampled standard normal noise vector (ε ∼ N(0,I)), and x̃ is the resulting corrupted data point.

σ represents the standard deviation (magnitude) of the Gaussian noise being injected. As σ increases, the additive noise term σε becomes larger in magnitude, causing x̃ to deviate further from the original x, resulting in more severe corruption of the data.

The corrupted data distribution is the conditional probability distribution of noisy data points x̃ given a clean data point x and noise level σ. The noise standard deviation σ controls the spread (variance) of this distribution: larger σ produces a wider distribution centered at x, while smaller σ
A large value of σ should be used. Since x̃ = x + σε and the noise term σε scales linearly with σ, increasing σ increases the magnitude of noise added to x, producing a more heavily corrupted image.
S_true(x̃ | x, σ) = −ϵ/σ² (or equivalently −(x − x̃)/σ²)
Loss = (1/2)||S_θ(x̃, σ) − S_true(x̃ | x, σ)||²₂

The λ(σ) = σ² weighting factor ensures that higher noise levels (which are harder to predict accurately and contribute noisily to training) are not ignored, while preventing lower noise levels from dominating the gradient due to smaller absolute errors. Without weighting, the network would prioritiz

The unweighted NCSN loss is Loss = ½||S_θ(x̃, σ) + ϵ/σ²||²₂, which treats all noise levels equally. The weighted NCSN loss is L(θ) = λ(σ)σ||S_θ(x̃, σ) + ϵ||²₂ with λ(σ) = σ², introducing a total weighting factor of σ³. The weighting factor λ(σ) = σ² is necessary because at high noise levels, the sig

The total weighting factor is λ(σ) × σ = σ² × σ = σ³. At very small noise levels (e.g., σ = 0.01), the weighting factor σ³ ≈ 10⁻⁶ makes gradients nearly vanishing, preventing the network from over-fitting to residual noise in nearly-clean data. At very large noise levels (e.g., σ = 1.0), the weighti

Using L_i = λ(σᵢ) × σᵢ × squared error: L₁ = (0.1)² × 0.1 × 0.04 = 0.001 × 0.1 × 0.04 = 0.00004. L₂ = (0.5)² × 0.5 × 0.04 = 0.25 × 0.5 × 0.04 = 0.005. L₃ = (1.0)² × 1.0 × 0.04 = 1.0 × 1.0 × 0.04 = 0.04. The noise level σ₃ = 1.0 contributes the most (0.04 out of total ≈ 0.045). Although all three noi

Under the unweighted loss, gradients at high noise levels are naturally much larger than at low noise levels, because at high σ the noise magnitude ||ϵ|| is large and the signal-to-noise ratio is low, producing larger squared errors. The network would learn faster at high noise levels and develop po

The unweighted loss produces small gradients at low noise levels because the true score magnitude ||−ϵ/σ²|| shrinks dramatically as σ → 0 (noise becomes tiny). At high noise levels, gradients are large and the network learns rapidly. During training, the network therefore develops strong score estim

A complete answer should explain that q(x̃|x) = N(x + σϵ, σ²I), and the score function ∇_x̃ log q(x̃|x) measures the gradient of the log-probability. Since the conditional distribution is centered at x, the score points back toward x, i.e., in the direction -(x̃ - x)/σ² = -ϵ/σ². The variance σ² appe

The weighted loss is L(θ) = σ² ||S_θ(x̃, σ) + ϵ/σ²||². This can be rewritten as L(θ) = ||σ S_θ(x̃, σ) + ϵ/σ||². The weighting by σ² ensures that gradient contributions are balanced across noise levels: at high σ, the true score ϵ/σ² is small in magnitude, so multiplying by σ² amplifies the loss and

The step size α_i = ϵ_step · σ_i²/σ_L² scales with σ_i² because the score magnitude ||S_θ(x̃, σ_i)|| is itself inversely proportional to σ_i (since S_θ ∝ -ϵ/σ_i²). At high noise levels, the score is weak, so a larger step size compensates and ensures meaningful updates; at low noise levels, the scor

The denominator √(1 - ᾱ_t) is the standard deviation of the noise distribution at step t under DDPM's schedule. Since q(x_t|x_0) = √(ᾱ_t) x_0 + √(1 - ᾱ_t) ϵ, the accumulated noise has variance 1 - ᾱ_t. The score ∇_x_t log q(x_t|x_0) = -(x_t - √(ᾱ_t) x_0)/(1 - ᾱ_t), which equals -ϵ/(1 - ᾱ_t) when x_t

turns.prompt: make it 30 marks total, 10 per section
turns.text: On it — restructuring to 30 marks total (10 per section). Updating the exam now.

refine.instruction: Revise the active exam to **30 marks total, distributed as 10 marks per section** (Score-Based Models, Diffusion/DDPM, and Flow Matching). Reduce question count and mark allocations proportionally while keeping depth. Keep the 90-minute duration and advanced level. Maintain the three-section structure grounded in the three selected note files (05_ncsn_score_based_models_notes.pdf, 06_diffusion_ddpm_ddim_notes.pdf, 07_flow_matching_notes.pdf).

turns.prompt: the langevin question in section 1 is too easy, make it harder, dont touch the rest
turns.text: On it — sharpening the Annealed Langevin Dynamics question in Section 1. Updating now.

refine.instruction: In Section A (Annealed Langevin Dynamics), replace the current Langevin question with a harder version that requires students to: (1) derive or justify why the step size must scale as α_i = ε_step · σ_i²/σ_L², (2) analyze what happens to gradient flow and sample quality at extreme noise levels if step sizing is held constant instead, and (3) connect adaptive step sizing to the sampling trajectory quality across the full denoising schedule. The question should demand synthesis across noise schedules, numerical stability, and the relationship between score magnitude and noise level. Keep the 3-mark allocation and the overall structure of Section A intact.
refine.summary: Replaced Langevin ALD question with a harder version demanding step-size derivation and critical analysis of adaptive vs. constant scheduling.

turns.prompt: add a ddim eta question to the diffusion section
turns.text: Adding a DDIM eta question to the diffusion section now.

refine.instruction: In Section B (Diffusion Models), add a new short_answer question (3 marks) on DDIM eta (the stochasticity parameter). The question should ask students to explain what eta controls in the DDIM sampler, how it interpolates between deterministic (eta=0) and stochastic (eta=1) sampling, and what practical trade-off it represents. Place it after the existing diffusion questions but before the section ends. Do not modify any other sections or questions.
refine.summary: Added DDIM eta question to diffusion section (3 marks)

turns.prompt: in section 3 swap one q for the linear interpolant and target velocity
turns.text: Swapping one question in Section 3 for linear interpolant and target velocity concepts. Updating the exam now.

refine.instruction: In Section 3 (Flow Matching), replace one of the existing questions with a new short-answer question (3–4 marks) on the linear interpolant ψ(t) = (1−t)x₀ + tx₁ and target velocity v_t in flow matching. The question should ask students to derive or explain how the target velocity v_t relates to the linear interpolant's time derivative, or to compute v_t given specific boundary conditions. Keep the total section at 10 marks and maintain the question count/difficulty balance.
refine.summary: Replaced one Section 3 question with linear interpolant & target velocity focus

refine.instruction: Reweight the exam sections: Section 1 (Score-Based Models) stays 10 marks. Section 2 (Diffusion & DDIM) increase to 12 marks by adding or expanding questions. Section 3 (Flow Matching) decrease to 8 marks by removing or shortening one question. Keep total at 30 marks. Preserve all prior edits: the harder Langevin question, the new DDIM eta question, and the linear interpolant/target velocity question in Section 3.

turns.prompt: which files did u use in the end? list the final documents
turns.text:
```
Files used to build this exam:

1. 05_ncsn_score_based_models_notes.pdf
   → Section 1 (Score-Based Models): forward process, true score, 
     weighted loss, ALD step size & dynamics

2. 06_diffusion_ddpm_ddim_notes.pdf
   → Section 2 (Diffusion & DDIM): forward process, variance schedule,
     DDPM posterior, DDIM eta parameter, deterministic sampling

3. 07_flow_matching_notes.pdf
   → Section 3 (Flow Matching): linear interpolation, target velocity,
     conditional flow matching, ODE sampler

All three files were grounded in the selected sources throughout.
```
